chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: Hello guys, my name is Nitish and you are welcome to my YouTube channel. So, we are continuing our 100 days of deep learning course. So, we had studied these multilayer perceptrons. We had taken their basic intuition, we had studied their notation, and how does it have a prediction. We had covered these three videos.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: And after that we had to study how they are trained using back propagation. But then I decided that I will first show you some artificial neural networks on some real-world data sets using the Keras library. Because I believe that once you have seen the practical first, then it becomes a little easier to understand the theory of that thing. So, that is why I had picked up a real-world data set in the last video, customer churn prediction. And we had made a binary classification model on it, which was a ANN.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: Now, we are going to do something similar in today's video. Today, we will take a different data set, which is the very famous MNIST data set, where you will have a photograph of digits. And you will have to make a prediction as to which digit is there in that photo, from 0 to 9. Now, the reason to raise this problem is that this is a multi-class classification problem.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: It is not binary, it is multi-class. So, I have to show you how a multi-class classification problem is solved using ANN. So, today's video will be all about that. The end goal is not to reach the best accuracy. The end goal is to learn how to solve a multi-class classification problem using an ANN.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: Again, we will use the Keras library. And that's it for the introduction. Let's start the video. So, guys, as I told you that in this video, we will work on MNIST data set. What is MNIST data set? It's basically a collection of 70,000 images like this.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: We will have a photograph of many people's handwritten digits. Exactly 70,000 photographs. And you will see one digit in every photo that someone has written by hand. And his photo has been taken. These images are very low resolution images.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: As you can see, their resolution is only 28x28. Means 28 pixels in this direction and 28 pixels in this direction. Means in total, there are 784 pixel values in this image. Now what we have to do is we have to train the data on 70,000 images. And then we have to make a model.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: If you give a new image to it, it will tell you which digit is in that photo. Now this data set is very famous. If you have worked in machine learning, then I guess you must have got this data set to work. So we will make an ANN prediction on it. So the idea is very simple.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: In our neural network architecture, we will start with let's say this architecture where there will be 128 nodes. And then in the output, there would be 10 nodes. This will be the output layer. Now you might ask why there are 10 nodes here. Generally, we put only one.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: The reason for this is that this is a multi-class classification problem. Means the categories here are 0, 1, 2 to 9. There are 10 categories. So whenever we are working with multi-class classification problem, you put as many nodes in the output node as your classes are.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: And then you consider the highest probability of any of these as the output. So I will tell you that our initial architecture will be something like this. In our input layer, a total of 784 inputs will come. One input for each pixel. After that, it will go to our next layer.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: Hidden layer in which there will be 128 nodes. And then in the last, it will go to the output node where there will be 10 nodes. So you can already sense that this time there will be a lot of weights and biases. Like if you talk about this layer, then 784 multiplied by 128 plus 128. So many weights and biases will be here.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: If you talk about this one, then 128 multiplied by 10 plus 10 will be in this layer. If you want, you can introduce more hidden layers if you feel that your accuracy is not improving. But this is the problem. Like this is the summary. We have digits inside a photograph.

chunks.title: Handwritten Digit Classification using ANN | MNIST Dataset
chunks.text: We will extract the pixel value of this photograph. And we will send that pixel value to an input node. Which we will send to a hidden node. And then we will send it to our output. And whatever result will come, we will show it to the user.

text: Hello guys, my name is Nitish and you are welcome to my YouTube channel. So, we are continuing our 100 days of deep learning course. So, we had studied these multilayer perceptrons. We had taken their basic intuition, we had studied their notation, and how does it have a prediction. We had covered these three videos. And after that we had to study how they are trained using back propagation. But then I decided that I will first show you some artificial neural networks on some real-world data sets using the Keras library. Because I believe that once you have seen the practical first, then it becomes a little easier to understand the theory of that thing. So, that is why I had picked up a real-world data set in the last video, customer churn prediction. And we had made a binary classification model on it, which was a ANN. Now, we are going to do something similar in today's video. Today, we will take a different data set, which is the very famous MNIST data set, where you will have a photograph of digits. And you will have to make a prediction as to which digit is there in that photo, from 0 to 9. Now, the reason to raise this problem is that this is a multi-class classification problem. It is not binary, it is multi-class. So, I have to show you how a multi-class classification problem is solved using ANN. So, today's video will be all about that. The end goal is not to reach the best accuracy. The end goal is to learn how to solve a multi-class classification problem using an ANN. Again, we will use the Keras library. And that's it for the introduction. Let's start the video. So, guys, as I told you that in this video, we will work on MNIST data set. What is MNIST data set? It's basically a collection of 70,000 images like this. We will have a photograph of many people's handwritten digits. Exactly 70,000 photographs. And you will see one digit in every photo that someone has written by hand. And his photo has been taken. These images are very low resolution images. As you can see, their resolution is only 28x28. Means 28 pixels in this direction and 28 pixels in this direction. Means in total, there are 784 pixel values in this image. Now what we have to do is we have to train the data on 70,000 images. And then we have to make a model. If you give a new image to it, it will tell you which digit is in that photo. Now this data set is very famous. If you have worked in machine learning, then I guess you must have got this data set to work. So we will make an ANN prediction on it. So the idea is very simple. In our neural network architecture, we will start with let's say this architecture where there will be 128 nodes. And then in the output, there would be 10 nodes. This will be the output layer. Now you might ask why there are 10 nodes here. Generally, we put only one. The reason for this is that this is a multi-class classification problem. Means the categories here are 0, 1, 2 to 9. There are 10 categories. So whenever we are working with multi-class classification problem, you put as many nodes in the output node as your classes are. And then you consider the highest probability of any of these as the output. So I will tell you that our initial architecture will be something like this. In our input layer, a total of 784 inputs will come. One input for each pixel. After that, it will go to our next layer. Hidden layer in which there will be 128 nodes. And then in the last, it will go to the output node where there will be 10 nodes. So you can already sense that this time there will be a lot of weights and biases. Like if you talk about this layer, then 784 multiplied by 128 plus 128. So many weights and biases will be here. If you talk about this one, then 128 multiplied by 10 plus 10 will be in this layer. If you want, you can introduce more hidden layers if you feel that your accuracy is not improving. But this is the problem. Like this is the summary. We have digits inside a photograph. We will extract the pixel value of this photograph. And we will send that pixel value to an input node. Which we will send to a hidden node. And then we will send it to our output. And whatever result will come, we will show it to the user. So now I will show you how our data is. So we will work in Google Colab. And here I have already imported these libraries. Now the good thing is that this MNIST dataset is so famous that Keras has already included it as a built-in dataset. So I don't need to bring it from anywhere. I just have to write this code, keras.datasets.mnist. And there is a function inside it, load data, which will provide you with a dataset in exactly this format. So X-Train and Y-Train will be separate. X-Test and Y-Test will be separate. So I don't have to do anything. I don't even have to split the train test. Now I will show you what is inside X-Train. So if you print X-Train. You would see that these are your images. So the data here is not in the form of actual images. It is in the form of pixel value. So what happened is that this X-Train is basically if you take out its shape. So you will find that this is a 3D array. Okay, this is a 3D array. Where you have 60,000 images of data. And what is every image? 28 by 28 NumPy array. If you remove the first item from it. So you will get. A NumPy array whose shape is. 28 by 28. You can see this. So this is our first image. And if you look at the first image. So in this way, here are your pixel values. This is your first row pixel. Which has 28 pixels. This is your second row pixel. Here are some pixels in the middle. And so on. So I can show you. Here we have stored pixels row wise. 28 pixels of the first row. Then 28 pixels of the second row. Then 28 pixels of the third row. And then 28 pixels of the 28th row. This is how this data is organized. Similarly, if I show you. What is in the X test? So you would see. This is also a 3D array. Whose shape is. 10,000 comma 28 comma 28. So it means in our training set. There are 60,000 images of data. In our test set. There are 10,000 sets of set of images. So now if I show you. What is in the Y train? Basically our labels are. Which digit is inside each image? You can see this. So the first image is ours. Inside the X train. There is 5 hidden in it. 0 in the second one. 4 in the third one. And so on. Now if I want. In which we will pass this data. And it will be a train. He will understand. What kind of pixel value. Which digit is it? Okay, we have to make this. But before making it. We will do one thing. If you take any image. The values in it. They are like this. Between 0 to 255. Okay, so we have to do the first thing. That we have our array. As in this array. It has to be between 0 to 1. All its values. Why am I saying this? Because whenever you train neural networks. The values are in a similar range. Your weights. Come out as soon as possible. And the convergence is fast. We will discuss this later. Why does this happen? But just understand at this point. It's very very important. That your numbers are in a similar range. So what will we do? We will divide all these values. 255 which is the maximum value. We will divide it. What will happen with it? The maximum value of 255. When you divide it. Your output will be 1. And your minimum value is 0. When you divide it by 255. Your value will be 0. So what will happen? All your pixel values. Will come in the range of 0 to 1. And we have to do that.
